context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m working on the city case study data table rn and kinda stuck on what counts as the main categories. can u help me figure out what info i def need for the assignment?
exchanges.tutor: Great that you're getting started! Before I help, have you read through the assignment description carefully? The required categories are actually spelled out there. What does it say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a straightforward clarification question about the assignment requirements. The assignment explicitly lists the required categories, so I should guide the student to find this information themselves rather than listing it for them. This keeps the student engaged and reading the assignment carefully, which is a good habit.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think it says stuff like location, population, area, income, GDP, and climate zone, but i’m not sure if i’m missing anything. is race/ethnicity breakdown also required?
exchanges.tutor: You're close! Go back and compare your list side by side with what the assignment says — you'll be able to answer your own question about race/ethnicity that way. Does your list match exactly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has mostly recalled the required categories correctly and is checking on race/ethnicity. They are very close to having the full list — I should confirm they're on the right track and nudge them to verify their own list against the assignment rather than just confirming for them. This keeps them engaged with the source material.

exchanges.student: oh wait, no, i did miss that lol. yeah it does say population race/ethnicity breakdown too.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch! So now that you have the full list, have you chosen your city yet? And do you have a sense of where you might look to find this data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all the required categories on their own, which is good. I should acknowledge this briefly and encourage them to move forward with the actual data-gathering work, perhaps prompting them to think about what city they've chosen and where they might find this data.

exchanges.student: i picked Boston, but idk where to find reliable data for all of it tbh. would census stuff cover most of it?
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking — the census is a solid instinct. But think through your full list of categories: do you think the census would cover *all* of them, or might some categories require different sources?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has picked a city and is asking a reasonable research question about data sources. Rather than listing sources for them, I should prompt them to think through what they already know about the census and whether it would cover all the categories they need. This encourages independent thinking about research methodology.

exchanges.student: probably not all of it, like maybe census for population and race/ethnicity, but not GDP or the Köppen zone? i’m guessing those need diff sources.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — good reasoning. So now it sounds like you have a clear enough picture to start hunting for sources. Give it a try and come back if you hit a specific roadblock!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and independently. They've correctly identified that different categories may need different sources. I should affirm this reasoning briefly and encourage them to go find those sources on their own, rather than suggesting specific ones for them.

exchanges.student: ok got it, thanks. if i get stuck on which source to trust for GDP vs climate zone, i’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds like a plan — good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up this part of the conversation and plans to work independently, which is the ideal outcome. I should encourage them briefly and let them go.

exchanges.tutor: Great, go for it! Reach out when you have something written up or if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to work independently. A brief, encouraging response is appropriate here.
The student is simply closing the conversation. A brief acknowledgment is all that's needed.
The student is just saying goodbye. A brief, friendly response is appropriate.

exchanges.tutor: Come back when you have your data table drafted — happy to help you think through it!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The conversation has naturally concluded. No further response is needed beyond a brief acknowledgment.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains why it is redirecting the student back to the assignment text rather than answering directly. No learning rationale (e.g., building independent reading skills) is offered.

When the student correctly identifies that census data won't cover GDP or Köppen zone, the tutor affirms ('Exactly right') but offers no method-level feedback on how to evaluate source reliability or what makes a source trustworthy for each category type.

The conversation ends without any reflection prompt. The tutor does not ask the student to articulate what they learned about finding data or how they reasoned through source selection, missing a clear opportunity for transfer support.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well on Socratic method and assignment anchoring, consistently redirecting the student back to the assignment text and asking guiding questions rather than providing direct answers. Scaffolding was appropriate and the conversation stayed on task. The main weaknesses are in meta-learning: the tutor never explained why it was using a Socratic approach, provided no method-level feedback on how to evaluate or select sources, and missed the opportunity to prompt reflection or transfer at the end of the conversation. Communication was concise and appropriately toned throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor performed well on Socratic method and assignment anchoring, consistently redirecting the student back to the assignment text and asking guiding questions rather than providing direct answers. Scaffolding was appropriate and the conversation stayed on task. The main weaknesses are in meta-learning: the tutor never explained why it was using a Socratic approach, provided no method-level feedback on how to evaluate or select sources, and missed the opportunity to prompt reflection or transfer at the end of the conversation. Communication was concise and appropriately toned throughout.
